sections.title: Use
rows: We use **so ... that** and **such ... that** to express a cause and its result — the first part explains the reason, and the second part shows the consequence.
The film was **so scary** that I couldn't sleep.

sections.title: Use
rows: We use **too** to say that something is more than necessary or acceptable, with a negative effect.
The coffee is **too hot** to drink right now.
We use **enough** to say that there is the right amount of something, or to say there is not enough.
She isn't **old enough** to get a driving licence.

sections.title: Be careful
items: **Too** is not the same as **very**. We only use **too** when something causes a problem.
**Enough** always follows the adjective or adverb: I'm not **confident enough** to speak in public.
Both **too** and **enough** can be followed by **for + noun/pronoun**: It's too loud **for** the baby to sleep.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Complete with so or such
exercises.title: A: Complete with so or such
exercises.instructions: Write so or such in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write so or such in each gap.

items.sentence: It was ...... a noisy restaurant that we couldn't hear each other.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is such + noun phrase, so the correct answer is "such".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete with too or enough
exercises.title: B: Complete with too or enough
exercises.instructions: Use too or enough in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use too or enough in each sentence.

items.sentence: This jacket is ...... small for me to wear.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is too small, so the correct answer is "too".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The ladder isn't long ...... to reach the roof.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not long enough, so the correct answer is "enough".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We haven't got ...... plates for all the guests.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not enough plates, so the correct answer is "enough".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: He didn't explain the rules clearly ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not clearly enough, so the correct answer is "enough".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: There are ...... many adverts on this website.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is excessive, so the correct answer is "too".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Is this room quiet ...... for you to study?

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: They arrived ...... early to enter the building.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is too early to enter, so the correct answer is "too".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.

items.originalSentence: The music was **so / such** loud that we left.
items.completedSentence: The music was so loud that we left.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: So is followed directly by an adjective (so + adjective).
explanationContent.grammarFocus: So + adjective. Such + (a) + adjective + noun.

wrongOptions.so: So cannot be followed by a noun phrase in this pattern.
wrongOptions.such: Such is not used directly before a lone adjective.

explanationContent.usefulTip: If a noun comes right after the gap, you usually need such.

optionContrast.such: "such" doesn't fit here → "so" does, because Such is not used directly before a lone adjective.

items.originalSentence: This phone is **too / enough** expensive for me.
items.completedSentence: This phone is too expensive for me.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The sentence shows a negative result — the degree is excessive.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Too + adjective = more than is good. Enough = sufficient amount.

wrongOptions.too: Too suggests a problem caused by excess; that may not match the sentence.
wrongOptions.enough: Enough means sufficient — it does not express excess.

optionContrast.enough: "enough" doesn't fit here → "too" does, because Enough means sufficient — it does not express excess.

items.originalSentence: It was **so / such** a strange dream.
items.completedSentence: It was such a strange dream.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Such is followed by a noun phrase (such + a + adjective + noun).
explanationContent.grammarFocus: So + adjective. Such + (a) + adjective + noun.

wrongOptions.so: So cannot be followed by a noun phrase in this pattern.
wrongOptions.such: Such is not used directly before a lone adjective.

explanationContent.usefulTip: If a noun comes right after the gap, you usually need such.

optionContrast.so: "so" doesn't fit here → "such" does, because So cannot be followed by a noun phrase in this pattern.

items.originalSentence: The room was warm **enough / too** to sleep in.
items.completedSentence: The room was warm enough to sleep in.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The amount satisfies what is needed — it is sufficient.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Too + adjective = more than is good. Enough = sufficient amount.

wrongOptions.too: Too suggests a problem caused by excess; that may not match the sentence.
wrongOptions.enough: Enough means sufficient — it does not express excess.

optionContrast.too: "too" doesn't fit here → "enough" does, because Too suggests a problem caused by excess; that may not match the sentence.

items.originalSentence: There were **so many / such many** people at the station.
items.completedSentence: There were so many people at the station.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "so many" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "so many" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "such many" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.such many: "such many" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("people at the station.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.such many: "such many" doesn't fit here → "so many" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("people at the station.").

items.sentenceAfter: a busy day that I forgot lunch.
items.originalSentence: It was **such / so** a busy day that I forgot lunch.
items.completedSentence: It was such a busy day that I forgot lunch.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Such is followed by a noun phrase (such + a + adjective + noun).
explanationContent.grammarFocus: So + adjective. Such + (a) + adjective + noun.

wrongOptions.so: So cannot be followed by a noun phrase in this pattern.
wrongOptions.such: Such is not used directly before a lone adjective.

explanationContent.usefulTip: If a noun comes right after the gap, you usually need such.

optionContrast.so: "so" doesn't fit here → "such" does, because So cannot be followed by a noun phrase in this pattern.

items.originalSentence: You are speaking **too / enough** fast for me.
items.completedSentence: You are speaking too fast for me.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The sentence shows a negative result — the degree is excessive.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Too + adjective = more than is good. Enough = sufficient amount.

wrongOptions.too: Too suggests a problem caused by excess; that may not match the sentence.
wrongOptions.enough: Enough means sufficient — it does not express excess.

optionContrast.enough: "enough" doesn't fit here → "too" does, because Enough means sufficient — it does not express excess.

items.originalSentence: He worked hard **too / enough** to pass the exam.
items.completedSentence: He worked hard enough to pass the exam.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The amount satisfies what is needed — it is sufficient.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Too + adjective = more than is good. Enough = sufficient amount.

wrongOptions.too: Too suggests a problem caused by excess; that may not match the sentence.
wrongOptions.enough: Enough means sufficient — it does not express excess.

exercises.title: D: Rewrite with so/such/too/enough
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so it has a similar meaning.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so it has a similar meaning.

items.sentence:
The bag was very full, so I couldn't close it. **TOO**
The bag ...... close.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is too full, so the correct answer is "was too full to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "was too full to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The singer was very popular, so all the tickets sold out. **SO**
The singer was ...... all the tickets sold out.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is so popular, so the correct answer is "so popular that".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "so popular that", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
The street is not wide enough for two buses. **TOO**
The street ...... for two buses.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not wide enough, so the correct answer is "is too narrow".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is too narrow", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
It was a very cold morning, so we wore gloves. **SUCH**
It was ...... we wore gloves.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is such + noun, so the correct answer is "such a cold morning that".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "such a cold morning that", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
She doesn't speak loudly enough for everyone to hear. **TOO**
She speaks ...... everyone to hear.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not loud enough, so the correct answer is "too quietly for".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
There was a lot of rain, so the match was cancelled. **SO**
There was ...... the match was cancelled.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is so much rain, so the correct answer is "so much rain that".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "so much rain that", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

exercises.title: E: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one mistake. Write the correct form.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one mistake. Write the correct form.

items.displayPrompt: It was so a beautiful painting that everyone stopped to look.

cues: It was so a beautiful painting that everyone stopped to look.

items.answer: It was such a beautiful painting that everyone stopped to look.
acceptedAnswers: It was such a beautiful painting that everyone stopped to look.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Before a noun, so the sentence is "It was such a beautiful painting that everyone stopped to look.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: It was so a beautiful painting that everyone stopped to look. → It was such a beautiful painting that everyone stopped to look.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.Before: Check how "Before" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.a: Check how "a" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: He isn't enough strong to move the table.

cues: He isn't enough strong to move the table.

items.answer: He isn't strong enough to move the table.
acceptedAnswers: He isn't strong enough to move the table.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Adjective before enough, so the sentence is "He isn't strong enough to move the table.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: He isn't enough strong to move the table. → He isn't strong enough to move the table.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Time clauses set the order of events — match verb tenses to show which action happened first.

wrongOptions.Adjective: Check how "Adjective" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.before: Check how "before" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: There were such many people in the queue.

cues: There were such many people in the queue.

items.answer: There were so many people in the queue.
acceptedAnswers: There were so many people in the queue.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Many people, so the sentence is "There were so many people in the queue.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: There were such many people in the queue. → There were so many people in the queue.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Many: Check how "Many" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.people: Check how "people" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: The exam was enough difficult to make me nervous.

cues: The exam was enough difficult to make me nervous.

items.answer: The exam was too difficult to make me nervous.
acceptedAnswers: The exam was too difficult to make me nervous.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Excessive difficulty, so the sentence is "The exam was too difficult to make me nervous.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: The exam was enough difficult to make me nervous. → The exam was too difficult to make me nervous.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: The exam was too difficult to make me nervous.

wrongOptions.Excessive: Check how "Excessive" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.difficulty: Check how "difficulty" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: She played the piano enough well to win the prize. → She played the piano well enough to win the prize.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Time clauses set the order of events — match verb tenses to show which action happened first.

items.answer: We don't have enough space for another desk.

explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: We don't have space enough for another desk. → We don't have enough space for another desk.

wrongOptions.enough: "enough" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "too" is correct.

exercises.title: G: Mixed revision
exercises.instructions: Find and correct the mistake in each sentence. Rewrite the full sentence correctly.
exercises.studentInstruction: Find and correct the mistake in each sentence. Rewrite the full sentence correctly.

items.displayPrompt: The test was such easy that everyone passed.

cues: The test was such easy that everyone passed.

items.answer: The test was so easy that everyone passed.
acceptedAnswers: The test was so easy that everyone passed.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Adjective, so the sentence is "The test was so easy that everyone passed.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: The test was such easy that everyone passed. → The test was so easy that everyone passed.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.Adjective: Check how "Adjective" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: It was so a delicious meal that we asked for the recipe.

cues: It was so a delicious meal that we asked for the recipe.

items.answer: It was such a delicious meal that we asked for the recipe.
acceptedAnswers: It was such a delicious meal that we asked for the recipe.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Such + noun, so the sentence is "It was such a delicious meal that we asked for the recipe.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: It was so a delicious meal that we asked for the recipe. → It was such a delicious meal that we asked for the recipe.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.Such: Check how "Such" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: I haven't got too money to buy a new bike.

cues: I haven't got too money to buy a new bike.

items.answer: I haven't got enough money to buy a new bike.
acceptedAnswers: I haven't got enough money to buy a new bike.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Need more, so the sentence is "I haven't got enough money to buy a new bike.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: I haven't got too money to buy a new bike. → I haven't got enough money to buy a new bike.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — has/have + past participle; yet goes at the end of negatives.

wrongOptions.Need: Check how "Need" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.more: Check how "more" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: This tea is so hot to drink.

cues: This tea is so hot to drink.

items.answer: This tea is too hot to drink.
acceptedAnswers: This tea is too hot to drink.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Excessive heat, so the sentence is "This tea is too hot to drink.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: This tea is so hot to drink. → This tea is too hot to drink.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: This tea is too hot to drink.

wrongOptions.Excessive: Check how "Excessive" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.heat: Check how "heat" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: They aren't enough old to watch that film.

cues: They aren't enough old to watch that film.

items.answer: They aren't old enough to watch that film.
acceptedAnswers: They aren't old enough to watch that film.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Adjective + enough, so the sentence is "They aren't old enough to watch that film.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: They aren't enough old to watch that film. → They aren't old enough to watch that film.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail — choose who/which/that/whose to match the noun they describe.

wrongOptions.Adjective: Check how "Adjective" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: There was so many homework that I stayed up late.

cues: There was so many homework that I stayed up late.

items.answer: There was so much homework that I stayed up late.
acceptedAnswers: There was so much homework that I stayed up late.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Uncountable, so the sentence is "There was so much homework that I stayed up late.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: There was so many homework that I stayed up late. → There was so much homework that I stayed up late.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Uncountable: Check how "Uncountable" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: The instructions were too confusing for us to understand them.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail — choose who/which/that/whose to match the noun they describe.

exercises.title: H: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning. Use no more than four words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning. Use no more than four words.

items.sentence: The book was ...... I finished it in one day. **SO**

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is so + adjective, so the correct answer is "so interesting that".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "so interesting that", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The table is ...... to move. **TOO**

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is too heavy, so the correct answer is "too heavy for me".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "too heavy for me", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: It was ...... everyone took notes. **SUCH**

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is such + noun, so the correct answer is "such a useful lesson that".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: He isn't ...... for the job. **ENOUGH**

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not sufficient, so the correct answer is "experienced enough".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "experienced enough", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "experienced enough" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence: There were ...... the meeting lasted longer. **SO**

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is so many questions, so the correct answer is "so many questions that".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "so many questions that", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The room was ...... all to sit down. **TOO**

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is too small, so the correct answer is "too small for us".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "too small for us", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
